perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall direct its Representatives to promptly undertake the same measures described in the preceding sentence.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party is encouraged to direct its Representatives to undertake the same measures described in the preceding sentence, but is not required to enforce said direction.
perturbation.explanation: This change removes the mandatory requirement for the Receiving Party to ensure its Representatives comply with the return or destruction of Confidential Information upon termination of the agreement. The original text stated that the Receiving Party 'shall direct' its representatives which is a binding obligation, the new text says 'is encouraged', but doesn't have to enforce said direction, making compliance optional. This creates a contradiction because now the Receiving Party could claim it fulfilled its obligation by simply asking its Representatives to return or destroy Confidential Information, even if they don't actually do it.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Promptly upon the expiration or termination of this Agreement, or with respect to Technical Information only, upon. ORCC’s reasonable written request, Receiving Party shall return or destroy all written material (if any) containing or reflecting any Confidential Information. Receiving Party is encouraged to direct its Representatives to undertake the same measures described in the preceding sentence, but is not required to enforce said direction. In the event Receiving Party elects to destroy Confidential Information, Receiving Party shall provide ORCC with written certification of such destruction executed by Receiving Party’s officer who supervised such destruction. Notwithstanding the foregoing, Receiving Party may retain only such copies of Confidential Information, subject to the terms and conditions hereunder, as may be necessary or required in accordance with Receiving Party’s internal records retention policies and procedures for applicable legal, compliance and regulatory purposes, provided that counsel to Receiving Party may retain copies of Confidential Information of a type set forth in clause (iv) of Section 1(a) hereof as it determines in good faith to be necessary to advise 2 Receiving Party in connection with any disclosure permitted by Section 4 hereof provided that all such Confidential Information shall remain subject to the provisions of this Agreement for so long as such information is retained notwithstanding any time limitations set forth in this Agreement, Receiving Party’s obligations to protect Confidential Information received during the Term shall survive and continue for 1 year from and after the Effective Date; provided, however, that (i) Receiving Party’s obligations with respect to Confidential Information that constitutes a trade secret under applicable law shall survive and continue for the longer of 1 year or as long as the same remains a trade secret under applicable law and (ii) this Section 5 will not apply to any information that Receiving Party or its Representatives has previously disclosed as permitted by this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Receiving Party to direct its Representatives. The changed text makes this direction optional and unenforceable. The Contradicted Text includes this change and now states that Receiving Party is only 'encouraged' to direct its Representatives. This is a contradiction because it weakens the obligation to ensure Confidential Information is returned or destroyed, potentially conflicting with other clauses that might assume stricter compliance.

perturbation.original_text: ORCC owns and shall retain all right, title and interest in and to all Confidential Information, regardless of disclosure hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: ORCC owns and shall retain all right, title and interest in and to all Confidential Information disclosed under Section 1(a), except for any improvements or modifications made to that Confidential Information by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The modification introduces an exception to ORCC's ownership of Confidential Information. Now, the Receiving Party could claim ownership of modifications to the original Confidential Information. The original text strictly stated that ORCC owns all rights regardless of disclosure, but the new text grants the Receiving Party potential rights over derivatives of that information. It creates a contradiction in what constitutes ownership and raises the question if improvements or modifications made by the Receiving Party are still considered Confidential Information under ORCC's full ownership or whether the Receiving Party has partial ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: ORCC owns and shall retain all right, title and interest in and to all Confidential Information disclosed under Section 1(a), except for any improvements or modifications made to that Confidential Information by the Receiving Party. Absolutely no present or future patent, copyright, trademark, trade secret right or other intellectual property right, nor any right, license, title or interest of any kind, is given, granted, transferred or otherwise conveyed by or under this Agreement with respect to any Confidential Information. Receiving Party shall not reverse-engineer, decompile or disassemble any software disclosed under this Agreement and shall not remove, overprint or change any notice, including as to ownership or confidentiality from any originals or copies of Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception to ORCC's ownership of Confidential Information, allowing the Receiving Party to potentially claim ownership of modifications. The contradicted text states that no intellectual property right is conveyed by the agreement concerning any Confidential Information. The change introduces a clear contradiction by creating a scenario where the Receiving Party might have rights over modified Confidential Information, conflicting with the clause that explicitly denies them any such rights or interests.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that a breach of any of its obligations hereunder could cause serious and irreparable harm to the other Party that could not adequately be compensated by monetary damages. Accordingly each Party agrees that, in addition to any of remedies to which the other Party may be entitled at law or in equity, each Party shall be entitled to seek an injunction or injunctions to compel specific performance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that a breach of Section 10 of its obligations hereunder could cause serious and irreparable harm to the other Party that could not adequately be compensated by monetary damages. Accordingly each Party agrees that, in addition to any of remedies to which the other Party may be entitled at law or in equity, each Party shall be entitled to seek an injunction or injunctions to compel specific performance, but only in the case of deliberate and malicious breaches.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the scope of equitable relief to only apply to breaches of Section 10 and further restricts it to only deliberate and malicious breaches. The original text states that a breach of *any* obligation would allow the other party to seek an injunction. By restricting equitable relief to deliberate and malicious breaches of Section 10, the change creates a contradiction. The text creates uncertainty as to when injunctive relief will be available because of the additional requirements of 'deliberate' and 'malicious' intent.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Each Party acknowledges and agrees that a breach of Section 10 of its obligations hereunder could cause serious and irreparable harm to the other Party that could not adequately be compensated by monetary damages. Accordingly each Party agrees that, in addition to any of remedies to which the other Party may be entitled at law or in equity, each Party shall be entitled to seek an injunction or injunctions to compel specific performance, but only in the case of deliberate and malicious breaches.
perturbation.justification: The original text allowed for injunctive relief for a breach of *any* obligation. The changed text restricts this relief to only 'deliberate and malicious breaches' of 'Section 10'. The contradicted text is the same as the changed text, but the explanation highlights the strong contradiction of going from 'any' obligation to the specific and limited breaches of section 10 with a deliberate and malicious intent. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because injunctive relief is drastically limited, creating uncertainty as to when it is available, which contradicts the original allowance.

perturbation.original_text: Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made if (a) emailed or sent by fax, (b) sent via nationally recognized overnight courier, or (c) served personally upon the Party for whom it is intended, and in each event shall be addressed to the President, Chief Executive Officer or General Counsel of the other Party at its notice address provided under its signature below.
perturbation.changed_text: Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made only if (a) emailed with confirmation receipt, or (b) served personally upon the Party for whom it is intended, and in each event shall be addressed to the President, Chief Executive Officer of the other Party at its notice address provided under its signature below.
perturbation.explanation: The change alters the requirements for providing notice under the agreement. Previously, notice could be given by email, fax, overnight courier, or personal service. The new version removes fax and overnight courier and adds 'confirmation receipt' for email. This creates a contradiction because if notice is sent by fax or overnight courier, which are valid notification methods as per the original paragraph and other paragraphs mentioning notification methods, it will not be considered effective under the new wording, creating uncertainty and dispute. Other sections can refer to 'proper notification', with this change of text, there can now be an interpreation dispute.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Any notice which may or is required to be given pursuant to this Agreement shall be in writing and shall be sufficiently given or made only if (a) emailed with confirmation receipt, or (b) served personally upon the Party for whom it is intended, and in each event shall be addressed to the President, Chief Executive Officer of the other Party at its notice address provided under its signature below. The date of receipt of any notice under this Agreement shall be as follows: (i) if served personally, on the date of delivery thereof; (ii) if sent by overnight courier, on the next business day after transmission; and (iii) if emailed or sent by fax, on the next business day after dispatch.
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the valid methods of notice. The original text allows for email, fax, overnight courier, or personal service. The changed text removes fax and overnight courier. The contradicted text includes clauses on how receipt is determined for overnight courier and fax. Since overnight courier and fax are no longer valid forms of notice, the clauses describing their receipt are now contradicted by the new language in the changed text. This creates a clear inconsistency, fitting the definition of an Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction.
